name: Image Size Audit
description: Analyze Docker image layers to understand what is taking up space and identify optimization opportunities.

steps.title: List images sorted by size
steps.description: See all images and their sizes, largest first.

steps.title: Inspect image layer sizes
steps.description: Break down an image into its constituent layers to see what each adds.

steps.title: Use dive for interactive layer analysis
steps.description: Run dive to explore each layer's filesystem changes interactively.
steps.tip: Dive shows wasted space, efficiency scores, and lets you browse each layer's file tree.

steps.title: Find dangling images consuming space
steps.description: Identify untagged images that are using disk space.

steps.title: Check total Docker disk usage
steps.description: See a summary of disk space used by images, containers, volumes, and build cache.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Image Size Audit
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Docker Disk Usage Summary ==="
docker system df

echo ""
echo "=== Top 10 Largest Images ==="
docker images --format '{{.Size}}\t{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}}' | sort -hr | head -10

echo ""
echo "=== Dangling Images ==="
docker images -f 'dangling=true' --format '{{.ID}}\t{{.Size}}' | head -10 || echo "None"

echo ""
echo "=== Optimization Tips ==="
echo "1. Use multi-stage builds"
echo "2. Choose smaller base images (alpine, slim, distroless)"
echo "3. Combine RUN commands with &&"
echo "4. Clean package caches in the same layer: rm -rf /var/cache/apt/*"
echo "5. Use .dockerignore to exclude build artifacts"

faq.question: What is a good target image size?
faq.answer: It depends on the application, but aim for under 200MB for most services. Go and Rust apps can be under 20MB with scratch/distroless bases.

faq.question: Why does combining RUN commands reduce size?
faq.answer: Each RUN creates a new layer. If you install and then delete files in separate RUN commands, the deleted files still exist in the earlier layer. Combining them in one RUN avoids this.
